s CURRENT AFFAIRS.

It is publicly announced that a Mr. A. J. Norris has
been appointed bandmaster of the D.M.P. band. Mr.
Norris is an ex-soldier and an Englishman. We

" believe there were three men in the running for it, two
Englishmen, Norris and Fawsett, and one Irishman,
Delany. We understand that the D.M.P. men are dis-
satisfied over the Norris appointment. The D.M.P.
band, we believe, is kept up by subscription, and it is
within the power of the D.M.P. men to stop their sub-
seriptions.  Norris was about thirty years in the Eng-
“lish army ; Fawsett was also a long time in the army,
and was employed as bandmaster by ** Ireland’s Own’’
band and by the Dublin Corporation Fife and Drum.
Delany is en Irishman, born and educated in Dublin.
He was & pupil of Robert O’Dwyer, the eminent Irish
 composer, and is at present, we believe, bnndmasha.r to
. the Glencreé band; he is to be called up for examina-
~ tion early next year bﬁ the Royal Academy of Music.
By the way, whilst the police may feel indignant at

the appointment of Norris, it is sfrange that a band.

~ n

S

called *‘ Iréland’s Own ’’ should have had an English
bandmaster. We hear also that the bandmaster of
York Street band is an ex-army man, and the James”
Street Band have an English bandmaster. The Father
Mathew and the Foresters’ bands employ Irish band-
masters.

There was a very strong personal attack on Lady
Aberdeen in Tuesday’s “‘ Irish Times " ; the first lead-
ing article is one long personal attack upon the iady.
In plain language it seems to charge her with corrup-
tion, with promoting, or procuring the promotion of
people for personal reasons rather than for efficiency.
Here is a sample:—

The country is beginning to realise that i (their
Excellencies influence in Irish affairs) is, from the
social point of view and from the point of view of
local government, a demoralising influence. It
has affected injuriously some of the most impor-
tant public services. It has interrupted the fra-
ditional, and, on the whole, successful, conduct of
official life. It has introduced mischievous ele-
ments of personal favouritism into that sociefy
which radiates from Dublin Castle.

That is pretty peppery, and the first thing that the
discriminating reader will probably conclude is thaf
some of Arnott’s friends or relations consider that they
have been badly ‘“ sat on’’ in one way or another.

This impression will be strengthened, by the following -
further quotation from the article: —

No more disastrous suspicion could affect the
public services than the suspicion that promotion
goes by favour and not by merit. This feeling is
rampant under the present »égime. There is, we
are compelled to say, ample justification for it.
The last two or three years have furnished us with’
a procession of promotions and honours in which
the only obvious claim of the successful candidates
was a lively intevest in the various causes that have
captured the Viceregal sympathies.

The Arnott crowd have evidently been sat upom.
For our part we don’t follow the course of jobs and
promotions and honours keenly enough to express an
opinion as to how they are distributed so far as they are
affected by the influence of the Aberdeens. We have
no reason to think that Catholics and Nationslists are
unduly favoured, however it may be with others; but as
we have said we don’t know enough awout the matter
to form an opinion worth having. The Arnott crowd
evidently think they have a grievance. But what
about the proposed dumping of Norway to the Secretary-
ship of the Post Office ~ The Aberdeens, we don’t s
pose, have anything to do with that, yet the “Ixi
Times *’ can spare none of its righteous indignation for
that insult to Ireland. Yet one of the sentences in ite
personal attack on the Aberdeens fits that case to &
nicety. It says:— .

In any case, the promotion of outsiders over the
heads of qualified officials who have laboured for
years in the natural expectation of advancement
is not merely an act of gross injustice to these offi-
cials: it is immediately and profoundly detrimentat
to .the whole public service. 4

Quite so; that just fits the Norway case; but the *“ Irish
Times '’ is mum on that. :

The Monaghan Board of Guardians have joined in
the protest against the contemplated Norway dump ;
so also have the Carrick-on-Suir Nos. 1 and 8 Rural
Distriet Councils and the Ca k-on-Suir Board of
Guardians. In tame little Birr of the militia a motion
of protest was defeated. Ts not Birr of the militia. the
place that used to be called Birr or Parsonstown? Mr.
Lowry, the Clerk to the Union, did not think the Guar-
dians ought to adopt a resolution of protest. By the
way, why should a paid official be allowed to join-in
the discussions of the Board?  This Mr. Lowry said
that there were thousands of Irishmen in the Civil
Service in England.  There are large numbers cer-
tainly, but what has that to do with it? Unfortunately
there are very large numbers of Irishmen in various
countries all over the world, for the tide of emigration
has been flowing strongly for many decades. = The
point is, is the chief position in the Irish Post Office, a
department that is to come under Irish control if Home
Rule is passed, to be given, over the heads of Irish
officials, to an Englishman who knows nothing of Ire-
land, and given only for two years? The brains of the
* Parsonstown ” Board of Guardians are evidently too
weak to take in the plain situation. . Does the Clerk
supply them with opinions as well as keep their
accounts? ~ We hope that other Guardians and public
bodies will express formally their protest against this
uncalled for insult to Treland’s capacity to conduct her
own affairs. What do the A.O.H. think of this matter?
The President, Mr. Devlin, M.P., has not, so far as
we know, uttered any public protest. Perhaps, before
he does so, he would like to have his hands strengthened
by the branches. The fear, real or feigned, that some
people profess for the A.0.H. *sometimes makes us

smile. TIf it is so powerful, where are the fruits of its
power? Surely it ought to be able to stop the Norway

importation anyway.

. an appointment which even yet, we hope, may not be

Our contemporary goes oni—-

1t is in our opinion essential that there should
be at the head of the lrish Post Otfice a man
trained in the lvish Sevvice, and who will pos-
Sess the confidence not only of the staff, but—
more important still—of the public. The Post
Office is one of the Departments which is imme-
diately to be transferred to the new Government
constituted under the Government of Ireland Biil,
and it is only reasonable to assume that in the
peviod between now and then the administration of
the Service should be in Irish hands, so that it
will be gradually preparing for the transfer. Yet
it is during that period that the head of the Ivish
Post Office is to be an Inglishman, utterly unac-
customed to Ivish life and Irish requirements (not,
of course, through any fault of his own, as, so far
as we know, he never did an hour’s duty in the
' Ivish Service), and therefore, in our opinion, ut-
terly unsuited for the discharge of the delicate
duties pertaining to the post during the transitionai
period. According to the official announcement
the appointment is only to last for two years. Why
if the selected man is the best qualified, he is only
to retain the post for two years, is another mys-
tery. If the expectations of the Government are
realised, at the end of two years, we will be on the
eve of the transfer to the Irish Government, and
it is at that critical juncture the English Secretary
is to quietly walk away and a new man (presum-
ably an Irishman) is to take over command. Is it
not commonsense to argue that the man who is to
fill the post of Secretary to the Irish Post Office
under the Irish Government two years hence
should now be appointed so that he may get into
contact with his staff and {horoughly understand
the details of his important post ?

Our contemporary also touches on the National finan-
cial side of the matter. And that is a very serious
matter for Treland, and it may be that the Norwegian
policy -is concerned with England getting a one-sided
financial settlement when the time for settling accounts
arrives.  As our contemporary remarks :—

““ If negotiations are to take place between St.
Martin-Le-Grand and Princes’ Street as to what is
to be credited as Irish income and vice versa, if is
surely necessary for the future of the Service that
the Princes’ Street side of the negotiations should
be carried on by Irishmen. With the best inten-
tions in the world an English official will not con-
duct these negotiations as an Irish official would,
and considering the supreme importance of the

_financial clauses of the measure, surely it is im-
portant that every care be taken to see that the
Irish Post Office Service is started on a fair and
equitable basis.

Who is I. F. Rowland, 8 Lorelto (Loreto?) Terrace,
Bray, Ireland?  Whoever the bounder is he has a
letter in the ‘“ Western Daily Mercury,”’ of Aug. 27.
He is evidently a Carsonite, for he writes, *‘ If all the
English. electorate voted for Home Rule it would not
malke the least difference in our determination to with-
stand it at any sacrifice’”  This Bray ‘ gentleman ™
says:—

i The Irish ave treacherous, and untruths come
as glibly as the mountain streams flow down. t¢
the sea this weather. How the dust is throwi
in the Englishman’s eyes, and how he believes
all their falsehoods, is marvellous.

Sir B. Carson knows the Irish, and hence he
will stand by his country to the end. B

Well, Carson may, or may nof, know the Trish;
but we would like to know who is this I'. F. Row-
land.

home to roost. An honorarium of £250 was given to

concerning Belfast Corporation extravagance and Bel-

head year by year. The Corporation do. not -ap-

The ** Irish Times * plays the political game so low
down, and with such a lack of logic, that we look vj.cn
it as 2 mere commercial advertising sheet, run for the
profit of the Arnotts, and ‘ edited’’ by a baclk-bone-
less person named Healy. Arnott’s profit-maker
would wave the green flag to-morrow if it suited the
commercial needs of his slippery paper.  We do not
take the paper seriously, except in so far as it is a symp-
tom of the political corruption and time-serving of the
class for which it may be said to stand. It is fre-
quently ridiculous, and, sometimes, unconsciously
amusing. It has a very silly and unconsciously comic
leading article in Monday’s issue. The unscrupu-
lous game of the Unionists is to pretend that an Irish
Parliament would be a dreadful calamity, particularly
for Protestants.  But it is hard, even impossible, to
always be on your guard, if you are weak and low
enough to start a lie, and wish to keep up consistency
with that lie. The odd job man who was allowed
to assume the editorial ‘‘ we ”’ in the first article, in
the issue of 2nd September, rather ‘‘ gave the show
away > on the nodding Healy and on the Arnotts, par-
ticularly on Lady Arnott, whose harangue we com-
We quote :—

The point we wish to make is this—namely,
that the mere establishment of a Parliament in
College Green, and the appointment of an Irish
Ministry, will not give the Irish people any more
zeal control over their own affairs than they enjoy
at present. If the party system extends to the
mnew Parliament, and if the doctrine of collective
responsibility applies to the Irish Cabinet, the

- Irish people will have no more direct control over
the doings of the Administration than they possess
at the present time. Indeed, we are inclined to
shink that they may have considerably less.

If the Parliament in College Green will give the
Irish people no more control over their own affairs than
they have at present, what is all the Carson
about. Lady Arnott, whom we may, ‘ptﬂ'haps, call
the proprietoress, ought to ask the saved '’ Healy for
an explanation. .

Tony Traill and the others who constitute the-Com-
missioners of ‘‘ National ”’ Education have come out
with o manifesto at which Arnotts’ anti-Irish paper
grabs with avidity.  To many people that fact alone,
without knowing any more about it, condemns qhe
manifesto, and stamps it with anything but the Irish
trade mark. The concern of these Commissioners
for *“ national education ’’ is, as John Bull, Junr., might
say, ‘“ a bit thin, eh!””  The National *’ Board have
done their best to Anglicise Ireland, and they have
made a sorry job of trying to educate it, and the public
have no sympathy with them. What about that pro-
mised second language ? Half the money that the Com-
missioners, present and past, misused would, if spent
on real Irish national education, have put Ireland on
its feet instead of on its knees.  Of course the *‘ Irish
Times,”” runs up the anti-Irish kopje and cheers old
Starkie and the Commissioners with the greatest anti-
Home Rule delight.  The Commissioners have made
a sad mess of Irish education, and even as anti-Home
Rule scare-crows they will not excite the Trish people.
We give one sample of the way in which the ““TIrish
Times '’ uses, for its anti-Home-Rule purpose, the
manifesto of the Castle-appointed Commissioners, who
as a body, are despised and distrusted by Irishmen :
‘‘ Nationalists are afraid to think for themselves;
Unionist criticism is attributed to political prejudice.
In these circumstances the Commissioners of National
Education have done the country a service for which
it should be deeply grateful.’ Of course, the old
jibe, ‘‘ Nationalists are afraid to think for themselves.””
We are all ‘‘ proxy-thinkers ! It is only such per-
sons as Sir John Arnott and the Belfast blackguards

who use their minds logically and fearlessly !

We learn that the O’Curry College, Carrigaholt, will
be kept open in the winter, and will be in charge of
Aine ni Bhriain, who has been there as a student
during the summer months.  TIrish classes will be held
in the evening, and the Gaelic atmosphere of the sum-
mer session will be maintained. Students of Irish
desiving to obtain lodging in the College may write to
Neili ni Bhriain, Ardanoir, Toynes, or to Aine ni
Bhriain herself.

Referring to our note on the Cookstown Horticul-
tural and Agricultural Show, Mr. John Glasgow, the
Hon. Sec., of the Show writes :—*‘ I make no defence
- of the Canadian Government Agent, further than to
say that he was not more in evidence than at any other
show in Ireland I believe, not even excluding those
at your own doors.  The majority of the Committee
and officials undoubtedly are Unionists. To qualify
for being a Patron or a member of Committee one must
have been a subscriber the previous year.  Every sub-
scriber of 10/- and upwards in 1911 is included in the
list of Patrons for 1912, while the Committee is fairly
and honestly representative of the remaining sub-
seribers.  Both the Clergy of your faith in this Parish
are subseribers, the name of the Very Rev. Canon Rice
appearing as a Patron. I'ew Nationalists were prize-
winners, indeed, I find you overstate the number. 1
have (for the first time in 23 years, and solely for the
purpose of this letter) carefully analysed the list, and
I find that we had in all 823 entries.  Of these, 75—
chiefly in the poultry section—are from exhibitors who
are strangers, and I put them on one side.  Of the
remainder, 735 entries were made by Unionists, and
13 by Nationalists, not quite one to fifty. =~ We had
ten judges; of three I cannot speak with certainty, as
they are from a distance, but if they are Protestants
your figures are correct. The payment of the men
at the gate has always been 5/- per man, never less,
and we had this year, as in every former year, one
Nationalist and one Unionist employed at each gate.”

There is a half-column report in the *‘ Irish Times”
of August 80th, of a special meeting of the Women’s
Central Committee of the Irish Unionist Alliance, and
practically all that report, exclusive of the space occu-
pied by names, is taken up by a verbatim report of a
speech by Lady Arnott, the wife of Arnott, of the
| * Irish Times.”” Who supplied this verbatim report of
| TLady Arnott’s speech—indeed we might ask as we are
I ab it, who supplied the speech? If it is the composi-
{ tion of Mr. Healy, the ‘‘ Saved " editor, we cannot
compliment him on- his effort. ~ But these are only
minor matters. The chief thing is that the Arnott’s
are making a good thing out of very inferior and very
commercial journalism in Ireland, and they are making
it by the aid and help of tame Catholics and non-de-
script alleged Nationalists. ~ What thanks do the Ar-
notts give the tame ones for their patronage?  Lady
Arnott’s address, as reported verbatim in the columus
of Arnott’s paper, is not without a certain interest, and

We all know what a difficult task it is to get the
hard-headed Briton to realise that it is sentiment,
inherited traditions, secret societies, and religious
difficulties, that are the forces which have formed
the past Irish puzzle, and how hard it is to geb
him to realise that that puzzle is quite capable of
fusing and igniting into a blaze of civil war and
bloodshed. Sentiment and inherited tradition are
difficult to analyse at a distance. The discussion
of religious difficulties is painful to many of us.
But there is one dominant factor which can be
driven home, and that is the power of the United 7
Trish League. We can all show how that League,
with the aid of Trish-American gold, and by bring-
ing boycotting and terrorism to a fine art, has
succeeded in getting the greater part of Ireland
into an iron grip, so much so that in many parts
of Ireland there is no such thing as a free fran-
chise. We can all show that Mr. Redmond and
his followers have two voices—one for British plat-
forms, talking smooth platitudes, and the other
for American audiences boldly proclaiming Separa-
tion, and that the ideal of ihe Nationalist party is
Treland a nafion.  Optimists are now telling us
that Home Rule is still far off, but it seems peril-
ously near, for on the one hand there is an English
Cabinet who, by the aid of time, the guillotine, and
the Nationalist vote, can pass through both Houses
of Parliament any measure of Home Rule that
they may be pleased to frame. And on the other
hand we have the British electorate dog-tired of
Ireland and Trish affairs, and bitterly resenting the
fact that Mr. Redmond is practically dictator on
all legislative matters.

There is one very black spot in Ireland, though it
is happily not of Ireland. The Belfast atrocities have
given Belfast a deservedly bad name all over the
civilized world. ~ But for Lady Arnott, the one bright
spot in the existing political horizon is what she'chooses,
like others equally as inaccurate, to describe as
‘“ Ulster.”” She goes on:—

The one bright spot on a dark horizon is Ulster
and Ulster’s passionate determination not to sub-
mit to the domination of the United Irish League,
when it becomes a glorified Irish Parliament; and
all we Southern Unionists can do is stand shoulder
to shoulder, and to strive our utmost to show that
it is no united country that is craving for Home
Rule, and that the welfare and prosperity of Ire-
land demand the rule and financial aid of Great

THE AGRICULTURAL COLLEGE, MOUNT-
BELLEW.

Our readers are familiar with the main features of
the. work carried on at the Agricultural College, Mount-
bellew, Co. Galway, by the Franciscan Brothers. The
aim—and it is never los$ sight of—is to train in the most-
profitable and most economical methods of qgricu]tu_re,
youths who will in due course be engaged in working
on, or as owners of the land. Only students who have
definite prospects in this direction are admitted. While
receiving a good rounding off to their general education
they are put through a course of training in farm work
that is in no sense merely demic. The course is,
before everything else,. practical. Great importance 1s
attached to the outside work on the College farm,
where the students are encouraged to give effect to the
lessons they have learned, in special plots set apart for
their individual attention. In this way the student is
familiavised with the actufl work of the farm, and
soon learns to apply, as a matter of course, the prin-
ciples which have been inculcated.

THE BELFAST ATROCITIES.

= OME of your readers whom I have met could
scarcely credit the atrocities deseribed in my
former communication. Indeed I might have added
that all the cases given could be confirmed by the
police reports.  The rowdies seem to feel that the
police authorities are in sympathy with them, and that
they will only be arrested when the police cannot help
it

Let me here give a word of praise to the rank and
file of the R.I.C. in Belfast. They would do their duty
fearlessly and without favour, but what were their orders
—do nothing. If we allow a little light on the conduct
of the authorities in the force what shall we find ? One
of these authorities was seen time and again going in
and out of the chief office of the Unionist clubs. No
doubt he had a right to do so; but if any R.I.C. chief
official was seen trotting to the offices of the A.0.H.,
what a cry would the civil and religious party raise ?
Here are two particular cases, in which the following
facts can be elicited by an investigation. Mr. David-
son’s Sirrocco Works, Bridge End,. were raided by
- rowdy shipyard workers in search for Catholics to chase

and murder them if desirable. ~Mr. Davidson heard
i beforehand his works were to be raided for this purpose.
8 (How did he hear it?) He reported the matter to
’ Mountpottinger Barracks. ~ What happened ?  The
- * works were raided that same day by shipyard rowdies.
| Catholics were abused and hunted from the works. The
police took no measures to prevent this occurrence.
They arrived on the scene when these demons had
almost finished their fiendish work. The back gate by
which the force sought entrance was closed in their
face. (Who closed the door, Mr. Davidson?) The
rowdies made good their escape through the front gate
and not one was arrested. Why were there not at least
two constables placed as sentinels to watch the way to
the Sirrocco Works ?  Why did the police ignore Dayid-
son’s warning. These questions must be discussed in
Parliament. 2

The second case.—A man named McCann, a quiet;
inoffensive man, living in Carew Street, had his house
wrecked simply because he was a Catholic. Naturally
one would think the police would keep a sharp eye
about this place. © But no. . The next evening the
rowdies came down and attacked McCann himself, he
- had to be taken to hospital and his life hung in the
balance for days by reason of the injuries inflicted.
Have the rowdies not reason for thinking that they will
be tenderly handled by the authorities when business is
- done in this slipshod manner ?

Though many Catholics have lost their work at
Workman and  Clarke’s, etc., the ' atrocities
have ceased for the present, but when' the fire-
eaters again spout your readers may guess what -
the Catholics will have to suffer. History in Belfast
repeats itself very frequently, and cases similar to the
following will recur. Young Delahunt leaving his worlk:
at the shipyard was attacked by a band of hundreds of
blackguards.  Safety for him was in the multitude,
otherwise he would have been killed outright.  Listen
to this fiendish cruelty. They attempt to strangle him
with his kerchief. They then take a sharp iron bar
and run it up his nostrils, splitting the nose open.
They then run it into his mouth and split his lip. They
run it into his face. His eye only escapes by the eighth
of an inch. They knock him down, they kick him,
and his sides, his stomach, his breast are one large
wound; only the crowd is so anxious to get at the
“‘Tenian *’ there was no hope, but they are so much
crowded together they cannot kick properly. The am-
bulance comes upon the scene, Delahunt is bundled
in, and the heroes cheer their handiwork and smash
the ambulance with iron ‘‘ nuts.””  We might give
similar details of other cases, but it would not be pru-
dent. For all those who have appeared in court to
give evidence or to make complaints must never appear
in the shipyards of Belfast, perhaps they would not be
murdered openly, but ‘“ an accident ™’ can very easily
oceur by things falling down on your head—on purpose.
The same could easily happen if their names appeared
in the LEADER.
After the shipyards,

perhaps the most wolfish
humans are the rope workers. Many atrocities have
occurred here which are truly blood-curdling. Do not
let it be said that the Catholics brought the trouble on
themselves; for as they are in such a miserable minority
they endeavour to go along as quietly as possible. One
poor girl, McManemy, was attacked by hundreds of
hooliganesses and beaten until she was dazed and
stupid, and then they took her over to throw her into
the Connswater River. The hooliganesses pulled her
bhair out in handfuls. They followed her in their hun-
dreds from the works to her home. They incited their
friends to wreck her home in Tamar Street, with the
result that her old mother, Mrs. Begley, and herself
were compelled to look for a new home on the Short
Strand and to live upon charity.

The hooliganesses beat the poor *‘ Fenians,’” men and
women, ab the rope works morning and evening as they
went in and came out. Their instruments of torture
were iron nuts with a hole, attached to a rope which
could be swung back and down with great violence
upon a Catholic skull. Some of these I can send Mr.
Editor if his friends would like to inspect them.

There is one other point I might add, the language of
these parties is of the most obscene character. . Their®
continual theme is priests and nuns, and their language
in this matter could not be published as it is so vile.
How do our Catholic girls control themselves? This
is true of many warehouses.

We will pass on to another typical case, the case of
Lizzie Curran. Tizzie was brought to hospital and de-
tained there. She was attacked by hundreds of male
and female hooligans, she was knocked down, kicked,
beaten, and her clothes torn into ribbons. For decency
sake a constable gave her his cape. Now, how were
the crowd assembled? They had congregated at the
Bridge, Deo Street, to stone a train supposed to be a

¢ Fenian”’ excursion train. No policeman here! But
the* ory is mlged *“ there Ygoes a Fenian,” and
poor Lizzie with her Protestant ‘“boy " set off

stable comes upon the scene, and Miss Connolly loses
sight of the heroines for a second while speaking to the
constable, but“unfortunately the heroines keep up the
running and run into the officer’s teeth and get ar-

There are. some cases of message boys being dis-
missed because they were Catholics. I may deal with
that subject at a later date. Again I assert the heads
of these business places could stop this by firm action
or closing down their-works. Millfield Foundry ended
disturbance in their works in a short time, and others
did the same. But these people are as anxious to per-
secute as the rowdy from the shipyard.

Now if the Northern Banks in the Catholic parts of
the country were relieved of all their deposit receipts,
etc., and if these magnificent buildings were turned into
white elephants, some of our drill masters might not
be so anxious for marching.

Another point will bring this to a conclusion. Why
do the Catholics of Belfast not form a company and
buy or build a warehouse or factory or several of them.
Then our workers would have some little independence
and there would be an opportunity of putting an end to
sweating. These could be undertaken without much
risk. As a matter of fact there are some businesses
largely managed by Catholics most successful. T hope
some of our Catholic business people will give this
matter consideration. There is plenty of money to be
had if the affair was properly floated and properly man-
aged.

J_\/I OS'I' things that go to make up beauty in nature
are here where the subject of this sketch has
dwelt for over seventy years, the last fifty-two of which
have been spent in the peaceful retirement of a con-
vent. A range of hills of beautiful contour stretches
to the west, gaining in boldness and grandeur as they
dip beneath thedistant horizon, till they finally culmi-
nate in the grandest mountain group in Ireland—where
the wooded Toomies look down on the matchless waters
of T.ough Lene. The hand of the spoiler has left here
more of woodland than unfortunately we find in many
distriets of our denuded country; innumerable streams
course down from the mountain heights to the fertile
plain beneath, and pay tribute to a noble river which
runs its course through scenes of rural loveliness to the
; the mountain nooks are gemmed with beautiful
ets; a scene fair even in this land of scenic beau-
ties ; and nestling in the midst of mountain, stream ‘and
wood, a convent of noble proportions. The casual
passer-by may pause, admire, and pass on, for this is a
picture fortunately by no means rare in Ireland. But
did he know there are two circumstances that would
irresistibly arrest the attention of any man with Irish
blood in his veins, for where the pretty convent ceme-
tery mestles under the shadow of a rarely beautiful hill,
there lies at rest a favourite niece of the great Liberator,
and the convent’s first postulant, who sat on his knee
when a child, who was often pressed to his great heart,
who listened ‘to the caressing tones of the magic voice
that filled the world with his name and the wrongs of
his country, who babbled of her ‘‘ Uncle Dan’’ when
the garrulous days of old age came on, and who was
always worthy of the line from which she sprang. And
side }];Y side sleeps a splendidly gifted sister of the daunt-
less Tipperary rebel, Michael Doheny, who roamed the
hills ]\omeless for love of Ireland, and in the lonely
nfn([)xinb]?n} fastnesses composed the pathetically beauti-
ul ballad :—

Tor thee I've tracked the mountain sides
And slept within the brake,

More lonely than the swan that glides
O’er Lua’s fairy lake.

The convent, stately us it is, may want the grandeur
of design and the architectural finish that characterise
the buildings of larger and wealthier communities, but
is it not something to harbour the ashes of an O’Con-
nell and a Doheny ?

Founded in 1839, tlie terrible drama of the Famine
swooped down on the district with particular virulence,
and old men speak gratefully still of how nobly the
good Sisters, out of their limited resources, ministered
to the wants of the stricken people. ILiike the mustard
seed in the Gospel story, it grew from very small be-
ginnings to its present stately proportions. ~Many able
and pious superioresses have ruled its destinies and have

been gathered to their reward, but not one was spared
to celebrate her golden jubilee {ill the present distin-
guished lady, the last, and perhaps the greatest, of a
ITong line. Of a reserved disposition, 1t takes more than
a casual acquaintance, and more than an ordinary per-
ception, to pierce beneath the surface of a somewhat
cold manner—that coldness that so often serves as a
mask for strong natures to conceal warm emotions and
feelings, and is so often misunderstood by the world;
for beneath that frigid exterior beats a heart as warm
and womanly and true as ever beat in human breast,
a heart ever open to every pitiful tale of suffering and
sin and misery.

A strict and impartial sense of justice combined with
a motherly heart, a rare combination of strength and
tenderness, gives to her grand and noble character a
proportion we too seldom meet with in our fallen and
erratic human natures. Born and reared in the locality
that has so incalculably benefited by: her life-long
labours, young and old hail with delight the approach
of her golden jubilee. Many trudged with her to school
in the long ago, many have fallen by the wayside, she
watched over the toddling steps of most of us in in-
fancy ; far over the world the thoughts of her countless
children will return to the old homeland on this auspi-
cious occasion, and pray that her days may be long and
happy yet. And (who knows?) beyond the bounds of
the spirit, world the long line of the children of fifty
years whom she led upward by her noble example and
devoted care, may share in her people’s joy, and watch
as we shall watch, and pray as we shall pray, that her
sun now sinking to the ¢alm of eventime may arise in
a day that is glorious and eternal. ;

Who can estimate the good wrought by a life such
as hers? As the stone dropped into the water sends
its ever-widening waves to break at last on the shore,
-s0 shall the souls she influenced for good during her

IMPLIFICATION per se is admirable. To sim-
'plify is to make intelligible. And simplifying
spelling in any language falls under the virtue of clavi-
ying and rendering easily grasped the tongue to which
it is applied, i.c., so far forth as making it clear and
seized by the eye is concerned. Thus, allowing the
doublé quality of perception by vision and apperception
by the mind to comprise the whole of linguistic under-
standing, simplification to the eye is already a share
of such total understanding.

Such clarification has been, for at least twenty years,
applied to the Irish language, which by reason of its
very age might be supposed eminently to stand in need
of it. For the image in Plato of Glaucus, the sea-god,
being covered less or more with marine aceretions, sea-
weed or molluses, is pregnant with meanmg.  That
image may be applied not unfittingly to our own Gaelic,
which has suffered many a sea-change during the ages;
which has been submerged by prohibitions and inter-
dictions; which by alien sway has been often compelled
for a season to go out, as it'were, with the tide. Such
changes have been compulsory and deleterious, result-
ing in a false and seeming gain here or a real but un-
perceived loss there. A comparison of Irish with Scots
Gaelic will show an old Irish Gaelic word or phrase
surviving in the Highlands and lost to its original home ;
& ringing on the counter of some Irish coinage of the
lips will show its mintage to be Saxon, the antipodes of
Celtic. These accretions, then, must be done away
with ; these Saxon, and plausible, words or expr:
must. be drummed out of our dictionaries, unless pos-
sessed of a good footing, and true, native equiva-
lents—deserters by sheer force of circumstan

refugees, let them come home, sure of full linguisti
citizenship in the land that gave them birth; so of the
words (Irish) kept in Welsh. This and such action is
part of the simplification, genuine as useful, whereof
every Celt would willingly see Trish seised. This and
similar procedure, in the way of cleansing, emen
adding to and recalling, is part of the true clarification
due to the Irish branch of the once all-embracing Celtic.
Thus the eye will take in and the lips give forth a clear,
simple, chastened diction, the tongue of the Gael, pure
and undefiled, nay, clothed in fairest raiment, even as
